text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
INTERIM RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, right kidney primary, status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. Prior imaging demonstrated mixed response to ipilimumab/nivolumab induction therapy -- pulmonary nodules decreasing, with new and enlarging retroperitoneal and pelvic lymphadenopathy on the first restaging scan (approximately 6 weeks prior). Patient now on nivolumab monotherapy maintenance. Interim restaging to reassess discordant response pattern.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, prior restaging examination (approximately 6 weeks prior); CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast, pre-treatment staging examination.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350) with acquisition in the portal venous phase for the abdomen/pelvis and standard chest protocol. Coronal and sagittal reformats were reconstructed. Images reviewed on PACS workstation with soft tissue, lung, and bone windows.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. There is no endobronchial lesion. The lungs are well-aerated without evidence of consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or air-space disease. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax bilaterally.

Pulmonary Nodules: Three previously described pulmonary nodules are again identified, all demonstrating continued reduction in size compared to the pre-treatment baseline, and further modest decrease or stability relative to the most recent prior scan:

- Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule: now measures approximately 0.7 cm in greatest dimension (previously 0.9 cm on most recent prior scan, 1.4 cm at baseline prior to treatment). The nodule retains its well-circumscribed, rounded morphology with no internal cavitation. This lesion continues to demonstrate an interval response to immunotherapy.

- Left lower lobe pulmonary nodule: measures 0.7 cm (previously 0.8 cm on most recent prior scan, 0.8 cm at baseline). This lesion has remained largely stable throughout the treatment course with a very slight reduction in the current examination, remaining well-circumscribed and without central necrosis.

- Right upper lobe pulmonary nodule: measures 0.3 cm (previously 0.4 cm on most recent prior scan, 0.6 cm at baseline). The nodule has progressively decreased in size throughout the treatment course and is now at the lower limit of reliable characterization by CT.

No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No satellite lesions. No miliary pattern.

Mediastinum and Hila: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. There is no pericardial effusion. The mediastinal contours are unremarkable. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber with atherosclerotic wall calcifications noted, consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk profile. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The central pulmonary vasculature is patent.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No pleural thickening, pleural nodularity, or pleural effusion. The ribs, sternum, and visualized thoracic vertebral bodies demonstrate no aggressive osseous lesions or overt cortical destruction. There are mild degenerative changes along the lower thoracic spine.

Axillary regions: No axillary lymphadenopathy.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous attenuation without focal lesions, cystic change, or enhancing masses. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. A few subcentimeter simple-appearing hepatic cysts are noted, unchanged in appearance from prior imaging and consistent with benign entities.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No gallstones, pericholecystic fluid, or wall thickening. The common bile duct is within normal caliber.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and morphology without focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreas is of normal morphology and attenuation throughout the head, body, and tail. The pancreatic duct is not dilated. No peripancreatic stranding or cystic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: The left adrenal gland appears normal without nodularity or mass. The right adrenal gland is absent, as expected given the prior right adrenalectomy performed at the time of cytoreductive nephrectomy.

Kidneys: The right renal fossa is occupied by postoperative changes consistent with the patient's prior right radical nephrectomy. A small amount of fibrotic soft tissue is present in the right renal fossa, unchanged from the prior examination and consistent with postsurgical scarring. No new soft tissue mass in the nephrectomy bed is identified. The left kidney is present, unremarkable in size and contour, with preserved cortical thickness and normal parenchymal enhancement. There is no left-sided hydronephrosis. No new renal masses.

Retroperitoneal Lymphadenopathy: The retroperitoneal and pelvic lymph node burden is the dominant area of concern on this examination and demonstrates continued worsening:

- Right para-aortic / aortocaval region: A confluent nodal conglomerate in the aortocaval space, previously measuring 2.1 cm, now measures approximately 2.4 cm in short-axis diameter. The mass demonstrates heterogeneous soft tissue attenuation with mild internal enhancement and a slightly irregular border, abuting but not definitively encasing the aorta or inferior vena cava. No definitive vascular invasion is appreciated at this time.

- Right common iliac nodal mass: The previously described right common iliac nodal mass, which was newly identified on the prior restaging examination measuring 1.6 cm, has increased in size and now measures approximately 2.0 cm in short-axis dimension. It demonstrates moderate enhancement and is located medial to the right common iliac vessels, with mass effect displacing the vessels laterally.

- Right obturator region: A new lymph node measuring approximately 1.1 cm in short-axis diameter is now identified in the right obturator fossa, not clearly present on the prior scan. This nodule demonstrates soft tissue density and contrast enhancement, morphologically suspicious for metastatic involvement given the patient's known malignancy and the trajectory of pelvic nodal disease.

The left common iliac, bilateral inguinal, and left external iliac regions do not demonstrate convincingly enlarged lymph nodes at this time.

Great Vessels and Bowel: The abdominal aorta and its major branches are patent. The inferior vena cava is patent without intraluminal filling defect, and no caval thrombus is identified (the prior renal vein tumor thrombus has been surgically excised). The visualized bowel loops are unremarkable without obstruction, wall thickening, or mesenteric stranding. No free intraperitoneal fluid or pneumoperitoneum.

Osseous Structures (Abdomen): Vertebral bodies and posterior elements at the lumbar levels demonstrate mild degenerative disc disease and facet arthropathy, appropriate for patient age. No aggressive lytic or blastic lesions in the lumbar spine or iliac crests are identified on this examination.

---

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable without mural thickening, filling defect, or mass.

Prostate: The prostate is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia in a 63-year-old male. No pelvic floor lesion is identified.

Lymph Nodes: As detailed above, progressive pelvic nodal disease is present, including the enlarging right common iliac nodal mass (2.0 cm) and the newly apparent right obturator node (1.1 cm). No left-sided pelvic or inguinal adenopathy.

Osseous Structures (Pelvis): No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified in the sacrum, iliac wings, acetabula, or proximal femora. No pathologic fractures.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. PULMONARY METASTATIC DISEASE -- ONGOING RESPONSE: The three previously identified bilateral pulmonary nodules continue to decrease in size or remain stable, consistent with a sustained immunotherapeutic response in the pulmonary compartment. The right lower lobe nodule (0.7 cm, down from 1.4 cm at baseline) and right upper lobe nodule (0.3 cm, down from 0.6 cm at baseline) have shown the most substantial reductions over the treatment course. The left lower lobe nodule (0.7 cm) demonstrates minimal interval change.

2. RETROPERITONEAL AND PELVIC LYMPHADENOPATHY -- WORSENING / PROGRESSIVE DISEASE: The nodal disease in the retroperitoneum and pelvis continues to evolve in an unfavorable direction, in clear contrast to the pulmonary response. The aortocaval nodal conglomerate has grown to 2.4 cm (from 2.1 cm on the prior scan). The right common iliac nodal mass has enlarged to 2.0 cm (from 1.6 cm). A new right obturator lymph node measuring 1.1 cm is now appreciated and represents further caudal extension of nodal metastatic disease. The aggregate picture in this anatomic compartment is one of progressive nodal infiltration despite immunotherapy.

3. NO NEW DISTANT METASTATIC SITES: There is no evidence of hepatic, adrenal, brain (not imaged), or new osseous metastatic involvement. No new pulmonary nodules. No peritoneal deposits identified.

4. STATUS POST RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY: Postsurgical changes in the right renal fossa are stable. No evidence of local recurrence in the nephrectomy bed.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This examination demonstrates a discordant treatment response pattern with continued regression of bilateral pulmonary metastases alongside progressive growth and spread of retroperitoneal and pelvic lymph node metastases. The emergence of a new right obturator node represents further anatomic progression of the nodal disease compartment. This divergent pattern of response -- pulmonary disease shrinking while nodal disease grows -- likely reflects tumor heterogeneity and may represent differential sensitivity of metastatic clones to PD-1 blockade. Clinical correlation with the patient's overall status and consideration of treatment modification are warranted.

Dictated and electronically signed by:
Department of Diagnostic Radiology
Body Imaging / Oncologic Imaging Section
